title: How Trauma Alters Pain Perception
researcherTopic: C-PTSD, the four F types, emotional flashbacks
metaDescription: Explore how complex trauma fundamentally alters pain perception by rewiring the nervous system. Learn why your body remembers what the mind forgets and how to begin healing.
imageDescription: Golden light filters through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene is quiet and still, evoking a sense of gentle mending and the beauty of a history that has been healed. The warmth of the light on the wooden table suggests a new morning and a peaceful beginning.
htmlContent:
The body’s memory of pain is not a metaphor. It is a physiological fact written into the nervous system, a ghost that haunts the architecture of our cells long after the original injury has passed, a silent hum beneath the surface of our days.

The Echo in the Tissues

When we experience trauma, particularly the developmental and relational trauma that leads to C-PTSD, the experience is not logged as a simple narrative in the brain’s neocortex. It bypasses the story-making mind and becomes a multi-sensory imprint, a constellation of unprocessed survival energy locked deep within the body’s tissues... the fascia, the muscles, the organs themselves. The nervous system, perpetually braced for a threat that has already passed, begins to operate on a different set of rules, a logic forged in crisis. It rewires itself for survival, and in doing so, it at its core alters its relationship with sensation itself, creating a new baseline for what is considered “normal.” A client once described this to me as feeling like their own body was a haunted house, where every creak and groan was a memory they couldn’t quite place but could feel in their bones. This is the lived reality for so many who work through the world with a history of complex trauma - a world where the baseline of sensation is not neutral, but is instead tinged with the faint, persistent echo of danger, a low-grade hum of activation that never quite turns off.

The Nervous System as a Distorting Mirror

Imagine a mirror that, instead of reflecting your image accurately, subtly distorts it, making you appear taller, shorter, wider, or thinner than you actually are. Now imagine that you have been looking into this mirror your entire life, and you have come to believe that its reflection is the truth. It turns out to be what a traumatized nervous system does to sensation. It takes the raw data of physical experience and filters it through a lens of past danger, amplifying certain signals and dampening others, all in a desperate, intelligent attempt to keep you safe. This is the world of hypervigilance and dissociation, of chronic pain and numbness, of a body that is no longer a reliable home but a source of constant confusion and betrayal. as the primary survival responses that become chronically activated. Each of these responses has a real and distinct impact on how we experience our bodies and the world around us. A nervous system stuck in a "fight" or "flight" response, for example, will be flooded with adrenaline and cortisol, leading to a state of chronic muscular tension, inflammation, and digestive distress that can both create and exacerbate physical pain. A system caught in a "freeze" response, on the other hand, may lead to a sense of disconnection and numbness, a feeling of being detached from one’s own body, which can be just as disorienting and distressing as overt pain.

When Numbness Becomes a Language

Learning to work with this numbness requires a deep shift in perspective, a movement away from force and towards finesse. Instead of trying to "break through" it or force ourselves to feel, we must learn to approach it with a gentle and patient curiosity, as if approaching a frightened animal. We must learn to listen to the silence, to feel into the emptiness, to trust that even in the most barren spaces of our inner world, there is life waiting to be discovered. It is a process of titration, of learning to experience small, manageable amounts of sensation at a time, so that the nervous system can gradually build its capacity to feel without becoming overwhelmed. This delicate process is a central theme in the practice of Somatic Experiencing for Trauma.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

Sit with that.

The Alchemy of Attention

This practice of mindful awareness, of bringing a spacious and non-judgmental attention to our inner world, is a form of alchemy. It does not necessarily make the pain go away, but it changes our relationship to it. It creates a space between the sensation and our reaction to it, and in that space, we find a new kind of freedom. We begin to see that the pain is just a sensation, a transient event in the vast field of our awareness. And in doing so, we begin to untangle the knot of physical sensation, emotional reactivity, and catastrophic thinking that creates so much of our suffering. What emerges is the path of dis-identification, of realizing that we are not the pain, but the awareness that is holding the pain. This is a foundational concept in many contemplative traditions, and it is a key to unlocking a new way of being with our bodies and ourselves, a topic we explore further in Mindfulness and Trauma Healing.

The Architecture of a New Reality

Healing from the way trauma alters pain perception is not about returning to a previous state of being, a mythical “before” that likely never existed in the way we remember it. The person we were before the trauma is gone, and the attempt to reclaim that self is a recipe for frustration and despair. Instead, healing is about building a new reality, a new relationship with our bodies and our sensations, one that is informed by the wisdom we have gained through our experiences. It turns out to be not a quick or easy process. It is a road of a thousand small steps, of learning to trust the body’s innate intelligence, of discovering that we have the capacity to create our own safety from within. It is a process of construction, not restoration.

This process of building a new reality involves a multi-faceted approach, a weaving together of different threads of healing. It involves the cognitive work of understanding the impact of trauma on the nervous system, as we can learn from external resources like Psychology Today, which offers a wealth of articles on the subject. It involves the somatic work of learning to listen to the body’s language of sensation, of tracking the subtle shifts and changes that signal a movement towards regulation. It is about creating a life that is not a reaction to the past, but an expression of our deepest values and our most authentic desires. It is about discovering that we are not broken, but have instead been reshaped by our experiences into something stronger, more compassionate, and more whole. We can find more information on the biological underpinnings of this from resources like the National Center for Biotechnology Information.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link).

The Topography of Inner Safety

One of the most significant shifts in the path of healing from complex trauma is the discovery that safety is not something that can be found in the external world. It is not a place we can go or a person we can be with. It is a state of being that we must cultivate within ourselves, a felt sense in the body. It is about learning to identify the people, places, and practices that nourish our nervous systems and to orient our lives around them. It is about learning to set boundaries, to say no to that which depletes us and yes to that which enlivens us. It is about learning to be our own best advocate, our own most compassionate friend, the parent we never had.

You have read the information. You understand the mechanisms. You can explain the theory to someone else. Knowledge without embodiment is just another defense mechanism. A sophisticated one, but a defense mechanism nonetheless.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my physical pain get worse when I'm stressed?

When you're stressed, your body releases hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, which can increase inflammation and muscle tension, making existing pain feel more intense. For a nervous system shaped by trauma, this response is often amplified, as the brain interprets emotional stress as a physical threat, further heightening pain sensitivity and activating old patterns of bracing and defense.

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

Wild, right?

Can healing trauma get rid of my chronic pain?

While there's no guarantee, many people find that as they heal from trauma and learn to regulate their nervous system, their chronic pain significantly lessens or even resolves. The process is less about "getting rid of" the pain and more about changing your relationship to it, untangling the physical sensation from the emotional charge, which in turn can change the experience of it.

What is a simple first step to reconnect with my body?

A simple and genuine first step is to practice noticing. A few times a day, pause and bring your attention to a part of your body that feels neutral or even pleasant - perhaps the feeling of your feet on the floor, the warmth of your hands, or the gentle expansion and contraction of your belly as you breathe. This gentle, non-demanding attention can begin to rebuild a sense of safety and trust in your body.

The path of healing is not a straight line, but a spiral, a dance of returning again and again to the places within ourselves that need our attention and our care. It is a path of learning to be with the full spectrum of our experience, the pain and the pleasure, the light and the dark, with a heart that is open and a spirit that is free. We can learn more about this process from a teacher like Kalesh, who reminds us that the goal is not to be free from pain, but to be free from our resistance to it.

A simple tool for working with specific tension points is Lacrosse Ball for Trigger Points (paid link).

Here is the uncomfortable truth: understanding changes nothing without practice.
